title: ODI Matrix: General Medical and Surgical Hospitals

sections.title: Market Context
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Market name** | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals |
| **NAICS code** | 622110 |
| **Market job** | Help Facility & Building Safety to maintain authorized access boundaries at emergency exits while guaranteeing single-motion escape compliance across all clinical zones, and provide real-time alerts on unauthorized activation events across the campus |
| **Total needs scored** | 28 (21 market + 7 product-related) |
| **Underserved count** | 16 |
| **Overserved count** | 0 |
| **Avg opportunity score** | 12.07 |

---

sections.title: Step 1 — Error Statements
sections.content:
### Job Map (Facility & Building Safety)

| Step # | Step Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Define | Scope which exits require monitoring across clinical zones |
| 2 | Configure | Install and commission exit devices without disrupting operations |
| 3 | Monitor | Maintain ongoing real-time visibility across all exit points |
| 4 | Detect | Identify unauthorized activation events in real time |
| 5 | Execute | Dispatch response and perform authorized bypass |
| 6 | Verify | Confirm compliance status on demand |
| 7 | Document | Generate audit records and regulatory reports |
| 8 | Maintain | Replace batteries and service devices with minimal disruption |

### Market Needs (from Step 06)

| N# | Error Statement | Step | Imp | Sat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | Minimize the likelihood of exit installation disrupting active patient care in adjacent clinical zones | 2 | 7.0 | 4.0 |
| N02 | Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized personnel accessing restricted clinical zones (pharmacy, NICU, psychiatric) through emergency exits | 3 | 9.0 | 4.0 |
| N03 | Minimize the time required to commission exit monitoring across all doors in a newly acquired hospital building | 2 | 7.0 | 5.0 |
| N04 | Minimize the installation time per exit door below 30 minutes without licensed electricians in occupied clinical wards | 2 | 8.0 | 5.0 |
| N05 | Minimize the likelihood of exit monitoring devices requiring visible cabling that violates infection control surface guidelines | 2 | 6.0 | 5.0 |
| N06 | Minimize the likelihood of fire safety inspection failures due to inadequate emergency exit security documentation | 7 | 8.0 | 3.0 |
| N07 | Minimize the time required for authorized personnel to override the exit alarm below 10 seconds during clinical emergencies without triggering a facility-wide alert | 5 | 7.0 | 5.0 |
| N08 | Minimize the likelihood of an unauthorized emergency exit activation going undetected for more than 5 minutes during overnight or weekend low-staffing periods | 4 | 10.0 | 2.0 |
| N09 | Minimize the frequency of false alarm activations on high-traffic clinical exit doors exceeding 2 events per week that require security dispatch | 5 | 8.0 | 4.0 |
| N10 | Minimize the time required to confirm all emergency exits are secured following fire drill exercises | 6 | 7.0 | 3.0 |
| N11 | Minimize the effort required to check battery status across all 200+ exit devices on a hospital campus from a single monitoring dashboard | 8 | 9.0 | 2.0 |
| N12 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless alarm transmission failure caused by RF interference from hospital MRI and CT imaging equipment | 3 | 9.0 | 4.0 |
| N13 | Minimize the time required to correlate an emergency exit activation event with a patient elopement incident record within 30-second timestamp precision | 7 | 8.0 | 3.0 |
| N14 | Minimize the likelihood of patient elopement going undetected through monitored emergency exits in psychiatric and dementia care wards | 4 | 9.0 | 3.0 |
| N15 | Minimize the time spent on manual battery inspection rounds for emergency exit devices across the hospital campus | 8 | 8.0 | 3.0 |
| N16 | Minimize the effort required to generate JCI-compliant 12-month exit event logs without manual data aggregation | 7 | 9.0 | 2.0 |
| N17 | Minimize the time for security staff to verify that all emergency exits were secured following fire drill exercises | 6 | 7.0 | 3.0 |
| N18 | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit device tampering or vandalism going undetected in low-supervision clinical corridors | 3 | 7.0 | 4.0 |
| N19 | Minimize the time required to produce on-demand real-time exit compliance status documentation for an arriving JCI surveyor | 6 | 8.0 | 3.0 |
| N20 | Minimize the likelihood of compliance gaps remaining invisible during unannounced JCI Environment of Care survey visits | 6 | 9.0 | 4.0 |
| N21 | Minimize the time from emergency exit activation alarm to location-specific security dispatch response below 15 seconds | 5 | 7.0 | 2.0 |

### Product-Related Needs (from Step 10 — injected from FIT file)

Injected with `job_step_number=0`, `job_step_name="product_constraint"`, `impact_category="reliability"`, `product_related=true`.

| PR# | Error Statement | Source Constraint | Imp | Sat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PR01 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless signal attenuation below reliable reporting threshold caused by reinforced-concrete hospital floor construction exceeding 3 barriers between device and receiver | C3 — Wireless Range | 8.0 | 4.0 |
| PR02 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered post-installation requiring unplanned repeater infrastructure additions to achieve full-campus alarm reporting | C3 — Wireless Range | 8.0 | 4.0 |
| PR03 | Minimize the likelihood of alarm event transmission failures caused by RF interference generated by hospital MRI and CT imaging equipment in zones adjacent to monitored exits | C3 — Wireless Range | 9.0 | 4.0 |
| PR04 | Minimize the effort required to replace batteries across all campus exit devices within a planned maintenance window without disrupting clinical operations | C4 — IoT Battery Life | 7.0 | 5.0 |
| PR05 | Minimize the likelihood of installation failure on non-standard door hardware — glass-frame doors, narrow lever handles, legacy panic bars — in pre-1990 hospital wings | C5 — Surface Mount | 7.0 | 5.0 |
| PR06 | Minimize the likelihood of door event logs containing data attributable to identifiable individuals without a legally established GDPR processing basis | C8 — GDPR | 8.0 | 4.0 |
| PR07 | Minimize the integration effort required to relay EasyWave alarm events into hospital BACnet or nurse-call BMS platforms without a custom protocol gateway | C6 — Protocol Lock-In | 7.0 | 3.0 |

---

sections.title: Step 2 — Satisfaction Recalibration (Incumbent Evidence)
sections.content:
| Incumbent | Est. Share | Mechanism | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wired electronic access control (dormakaba, ASSA ABLOY) | ~35% | Electronic locking + wired alarm panel | Reliable, BACnet-native, timestamped logs | 2–4 hrs/door installation, requires electrician, costly for retrofit |
| Mechanical door alarms (basic, no IoT) | ~40% | Spring or magnetic reed contact, audible alarm only | Low cost, zero maintenance complexity | No remote monitoring, no battery dashboard, no event logs |
| Nurse-station manual patrol | ~15% | Human observation rounds | Flexible response | Cannot detect real-time; hours-long detection gaps overnight |
| CCTV-based monitoring | ~10% | Video surveillance at exit points | Provides visual evidence post-incident | No automated alerts; no timestamped compliance logs; high review burden |

**Recalibration impacts applied:**
- N08/N14 (overnight detection): Mechanical alarms + manual patrol dominate → sat=2 confirmed; wired electronic minority at hospitals → does not lift aggregate satisfaction
- N11/N15 (battery management): Mechanical-only alternatives have zero remote battery visibility → sat=2/3 confirmed
- N16/N13 (JCI logs): No incumbent provides automated timestamped event logs → sat=2/3 confirmed
- N12/PR03 (MRI interference): Specific to wireless; wired systems unaffected but rarely retrofitted → sat=4 (a minority of hospitals use wired and avoid this problem)
- N04 (installation): Wired systems require electrician; product directly addresses → sat=5 confirmed

---

sections.title: Step 3 — ODI Scores (Deterministic)
sections.content:
Formula: `opportunity_score = importance + max(importance − satisfaction, 0)`
Underserved: `importance > 7.0 AND satisfaction < 5.0`
Overserved: `importance < 3.0 AND satisfaction > 7.0`

sections.title: Step 5 — Cross-Check vs. Feature-Market Fit (Step 09)
sections.content:
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| ODI avg opportunity score | 12.07 |
| Feature-Fit avg score (from FIT file) | 6.72 |
| Cross-check rule triggered | None |

ODI avg > 12 AND Feature-Fit avg > 4 — no inconsistency. This market has **strong opportunity** (12.07) and **strong product-feature alignment** (6.72). The three highest-scoring features (Automated Event Log Generation: 7.67, Remote Battery Status Reporting: 7.33, Retrofit Surface Mount: 7.22) directly address the top-3 underserved needs (N08/OS=18, N11/OS=16, N16/OS=16). Positive signal: product capabilities map onto the most urgent unmet needs.

---

rows.Value: Help Facility & Building Safety to maintain authorized access boundaries at emergency exits while guaranteeing single-motion escape compliance across all clinical zones, and provide real-time alerts on unauthorized activation events across the campus

rows.Description: Scope which exits require monitoring across clinical zones
Install and commission exit devices without disrupting operations
Maintain ongoing real-time visibility across all exit points
Identify unauthorized activation events in real time
Dispatch response and perform authorized bypass
Generate audit records and regulatory reports
Replace batteries and service devices with minimal disruption

rows.Error Statement: Minimize the likelihood of exit installation disrupting active patient care in adjacent clinical zones
Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized personnel accessing restricted clinical zones (pharmacy, NICU, psychiatric) through emergency exits
Minimize the time required to commission exit monitoring across all doors in a newly acquired hospital building
Minimize the installation time per exit door below 30 minutes without licensed electricians in occupied clinical wards
Minimize the likelihood of exit monitoring devices requiring visible cabling that violates infection control surface guidelines
Minimize the likelihood of fire safety inspection failures due to inadequate emergency exit security documentation
Minimize the time required for authorized personnel to override the exit alarm below 10 seconds during clinical emergencies without triggering a facility-wide alert
Minimize the likelihood of an unauthorized emergency exit activation going undetected for more than 5 minutes during overnight or weekend low-staffing periods
Minimize the frequency of false alarm activations on high-traffic clinical exit doors exceeding 2 events per week that require security dispatch
Minimize the time required to confirm all emergency exits are secured following fire drill exercises
Minimize the effort required to check battery status across all 200+ exit devices on a hospital campus from a single monitoring dashboard
Minimize the likelihood of wireless alarm transmission failure caused by RF interference from hospital MRI and CT imaging equipment
Minimize the time required to correlate an emergency exit activation event with a patient elopement incident record within 30-second timestamp precision
Minimize the likelihood of patient elopement going undetected through monitored emergency exits in psychiatric and dementia care wards
Minimize the time spent on manual battery inspection rounds for emergency exit devices across the hospital campus
Minimize the effort required to generate JCI-compliant 12-month exit event logs without manual data aggregation
Minimize the time for security staff to verify that all emergency exits were secured following fire drill exercises
Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit device tampering or vandalism going undetected in low-supervision clinical corridors
Minimize the time required to produce on-demand real-time exit compliance status documentation for an arriving JCI surveyor
Minimize the likelihood of compliance gaps remaining invisible during unannounced JCI Environment of Care survey visits
Minimize the time from emergency exit activation alarm to location-specific security dispatch response below 15 seconds
Minimize the likelihood of wireless signal attenuation below reliable reporting threshold caused by reinforced-concrete hospital floor construction exceeding 3 barriers between device and receiver
Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered post-installation requiring unplanned repeater infrastructure additions to achieve full-campus alarm reporting
Minimize the likelihood of alarm event transmission failures caused by RF interference generated by hospital MRI and CT imaging equipment in zones adjacent to monitored exits
Minimize the effort required to replace batteries across all campus exit devices within a planned maintenance window without disrupting clinical operations
Minimize the likelihood of installation failure on non-standard door hardware — glass-frame doors, narrow lever handles, legacy panic bars — in pre-1990 hospital wings
Minimize the likelihood of door event logs containing data attributable to identifiable individuals without a legally established GDPR processing basis
Minimize the integration effort required to relay EasyWave alarm events into hospital BACnet or nurse-call BMS platforms without a custom protocol gateway

rows.Incumbent: Wired electronic access control (dormakaba, ASSA ABLOY)
rows.Weaknesses: 2–4 hrs/door installation, requires electrician, costly for retrofit

rows.Mechanism: Spring or magnetic reed contact, audible alarm only
rows.Weaknesses: No remote monitoring, no battery dashboard, no event logs
Cannot detect real-time; hours-long detection gaps overnight
No automated alerts; no timestamped compliance logs; high review burden
